title: Crazy Malvika discovers Crazy Fibonacci function

content.problemStatement:
Malvika was getting bored of the usual Fibonacci problems, and decided to add a little twist to it. She defined a new function **f()** with the following properties:


She'll give you two integers, **A** and **B**. **f(1)** is defined to be **A** and **f(2)** is **B**.
And for all integers **x ≥ 2**, **f(x) = f(x-1) + f(x+1)**.
She'll give an integer **N**, and you have to find out what **f(N)** is. Output the answers modulo **10^9+7**.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer **T** denoting the number of test cases.
The only line of each test case contains three integers: **A**, **B**, and **N**, denoting **f(1)**, **f(2)**, and the query.


Output Format

For each test case, output a line which contains a single integer, corresponding to **f(N)** for the given input.
content.inputFormat: The first line of input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The only line of each test case contains three integers: A, B, and N, denoting f(1), f(2), and the query.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a line which contains a single integer, corresponding to f(N) for the given input.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, f(3) = 7, and so that is the output. In the second test case, f(3) = -6 and the answer modulo 10^9+7 is 1000000001.
In this test case, f(1) = 1, and so that is the output.
In this test case, f(2) = 1, and so that is the output.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the given recurrence relation f(x) = f(x-1) + f(x+1) to find f(N). However, since the relation is not in the standard form of a recurrence relation, we need to rearrange it to f(x+1) = f(x) - f(x-1). We can then use this relation to find f(N) iteratively, starting from the base cases f(1) = A and f(2) = B. We also need to take the result modulo 10^9+7 to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) — per query is O(N), total is O(T*N)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — we only need a constant amount of space to store the current and previous values of f(x)

code.Python3:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    A, B, N = map(int, input().split())
    mod = 1000000007
    f1 = A % mod
    f2 = B % mod
    for i in range(3, N+1):
        f3 = (f2 - f1 + mod) % mod
        f1 = f2
        f2 = f3
    print(f2)
